title: Monkey bread
subtitle: Pull-apart cinnamon bread baked in a bundt tin
excerpt: Small pieces of enriched dough rolled in cinnamon sugar, layered in a bundt tin and baked until the caramel binds them into a pull-apart loaf. The bread inverts from the tin in a mass of sticky, caramelised knobs. Pull rather than cut.
sourceNotes: Monkey bread is an American baking tradition, widely associated with the 1960s and 1970s domestic baking press, though its roots in shaped pull-apart breads are older. Mrs Beeton does not document it; the name and concept are American. This recipe follows the well-established American domestic method: an enriched yeasted dough, cut into small pieces, coated in cinnamon sugar and layered in a bundt tin with a brown sugar caramel.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely before freezing. Wrap tightly and freeze for up to 1 month. Thaw at room temperature overnight and warm in a low oven for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble the bundt tin the night before, cover tightly and refrigerate overnight. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan or conventional; bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the top is deep mahogany and the caramel is bubbling at the edges

glossaryTerms.definition: An enriched dough contains fat and sugar as well as flour and water. These additions slow yeast activity and also slow gluten development. Expect a longer first rise than a lean dough. The dough should feel soft and slightly sticky after kneading; it will firm up during proving.

content.text: Monkey bread is a communal bread. It is designed to be pulled apart at the table, piece by sticky piece. The
is slow to rise and needs full time. Do not rush the first prove. The caramel that forms in the tin during baking coats every piece as it inverts.

Combine 500 g flour, 7 g yeast, 50 g caster sugar and 7.5 g salt in a large bowl. Add 200 ml warm milk, 2 beaten eggs and 80 g softened butter. Mix to a soft dough. Knead for 10 minutes until the dough is smooth and slightly tacky. It will feel soft and pliable. Place in an oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth and prove for 1 hour until doubled in size.
Melt 80 g butter and 100 g light brown sugar together in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Pour into a well-greased bundt tin. The caramel will pool in the base.

Mix 150 g caster sugar and 2 tsp ground cinnamon in a shallow bowl. Melt 100 g butter in a separate bowl. Turn out the proved dough and cut or tear into roughly 3 cm pieces, about 40 pieces in total. Roll each piece in the melted butter, then toss in the cinnamon sugar. Layer the coated pieces into the bundt tin, building up in layers. Cover with a damp cloth and prove for 30 minutes until the dough has risen noticeably in the tin.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the top is deep mahogany and the caramel bubbles at the edges. The bread should reach an internal temperature of 90 to 92 degrees C on an instant-read thermometer. Leave in the tin for exactly 5 minutes, then invert onto a serving plate. If left longer, the caramel sets and the bread sticks. Pull apart to serve.

items.problem: Bread sticks to the tin and will not come out cleanly
items.solution: The tin was not greased generously enough, or the bread was left too long before inverting. Grease every crevice of the bundt tin with softened butter before adding the caramel. Invert at 5 minutes, not 10.

items.problem: Centre of the bread is doughy
items.solution: The pieces in the centre were too large, or the oven was too hot and the outside baked before the centre set. Keep pieces to 3 cm or smaller and confirm the oven temperature with a thermometer.
